edges.description: Diffuse intraocular inflammation disrupts ocular transparency and retinal function.
Cellular infiltration and inflammatory exudation can promote macular edema and other posterior-segment complications.
Infectious or immune-mediated triggers initiate the pan-uveal inflammatory phenotype.

nodes.description: Adalimumab is a steroid-sparing biologic option for active or inactive noninfectious intermediate, posterior, or panuveitic uveitis when inflammation is not adequately controlled with corticosteroid-based therapy.

evidence.reference_title: Adalimumab in Patients with Active Noninfectious Uveitis.
evidence.snippet: active noninfectious intermediate uveitis, posterior uveitis, or panuveitis

evidence.reference_title: Adalimumab in Patients with Active Noninfectious Uveitis.
evidence.snippet: median time to treatment failure was 24 weeks in the adalimumab group and 13 weeks in the placebo group

evidence.reference_title: Adalimumab for prevention of uveitic flare in patients with inactive non-infectious uveitis controlled by corticosteroids (VISUAL II): a multicentre, double-masked, randomised, placebo-controlled phase 3 trial.
evidence.snippet: Adalimumab significantly lowered the risk of uveitic flare or loss of visual acuity upon corticosteroid withdrawal

nodes.description: Vitreous haze, macular edema, or posterior segment inflammation can reduce visual clarity.

evidence.snippet: eye redness, pain, photophobia, floaters, and blurred vision.

nodes.description: Moderate to severe panuveitis often requires systemic and/or intravitreal corticosteroids to rapidly suppress sight-threatening inflammation.

evidence.snippet: require systemic and/or intravitreal corticosteroids and immunosuppressive agents.

nodes.description: Infectious panuveitis requires treatment of the causative pathogen before or alongside anti-inflammatory therapy.

evidence.snippet: Infectious uveitis requires systemic antimicrobial treatment.

nodes.description: Panuveitis can arise from infectious causes, idiopathic inflammation, or systemic immune-mediated disease. Etiologic classification is clinically central because antimicrobial therapy is required for infectious disease, whereas noninfectious disease often requires corticosteroids and steroid-sparing immunosuppression.

evidence.snippet: Uveitis is classified as noninfectious or infectious
37% to 49% are associated with systemic disease

nodes.description: Active panuveitis includes leukocyte recruitment into immune-privileged eye compartments, with inflammatory cells and mediators entering the aqueous, vitreous, retina, and choroid.

evidence.reference_title: Update on non-infectious uveitis treatment: anti-TNF-alpha and beyond.
evidence.snippet: inflammation within various layers of the eye.

nodes.description: Macular edema is a sight-threatening inflammatory complication of uveitis and panuveitis.

evidence.snippet: cataracts, glaucoma, macular edema, retinal detachment, optic nerve damage, and vision loss.

nodes.description: Methotrexate is a disease-modifying antirheumatic drug used as a steroid-sparing treatment option for posterior-segment noninfectious uveitis that can include panuveitis.

evidence.snippet: disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs such as methotrexate achieved remission of inflammation in 52.1% (95% CI, 38.6%-67.1%) of patients, and mycophenolate mofetil controlled inflammation in 70.9% (95% CI, 57.1%-83.5%)

nodes.description: Mycophenolate mofetil is a disease-modifying antirheumatic drug used as a steroid-sparing treatment option for posterior-segment noninfectious uveitis that can include panuveitis.

evidence.snippet: disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs such as methotrexate achieved remission of inflammation in 52.1% (95% CI, 38.6%-67.1%) of patients, and mycophenolate mofetil controlled inflammation in 70.9% (95% CI, 57.1%-83.5%)

nodes.description: Panuveitis reflects inflammation throughout the uveal tract, including the iris, ciliary body, and choroid, with spillover to adjacent vitreous and retinal structures.
Chronic, recurrent, bilateral, or vision-threatening noninfectious panuveitis commonly requires steroid-sparing immunosuppression such as methotrexate or mycophenolate mofetil.

evidence.snippet: require systemic and/or intravitreal corticosteroids and immunosuppressive agents.
